text: Hello, welcome on this video about laparoscopic total gastrectomy. My name is Benjamin Cadier. I'm a surgeon at St. Peter's Hospital in Brussels. And since it's Christmas time, I wish you all a Merry Christmas. This is a case of a seventy-nine-year-old patient who suffered from epigastric pain. The worker found a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma from the lesser curvature without any signs of metastatic disease. He went through preoperative chemotherapy.

The surgeon uses five trocars and positions the patient in the French position. The surgery begins with the dissection of the omentum at its colic insertion, carefully navigating adhesions between the omentum and mesocolon in a fatty patient. Blunt dissection is employed to avoid injury. Once the omentum is dissected, the posterior gastric wall is accessed, revealing tumor-related retractions. The dissection proceeds from left to right, reaching the head of the pancreas and station six lymph node. The right gastroepiploic vein and artery are identified with assistance traction on the pylorus, and blunt dissection with suction delineates the upper limit of the pancreas head.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic hook to precisely dissect the right gastroepiploic artery, completing the dissection of station six. A gauze is placed posterior to the pylorus, followed by anterior dissection exposing station five. A 12mm trocar is inserted for stapling the first part of the duodenum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the territory of the right gastric artery, controlling a small branch leading to the duodenum. The dissection proceeds to the hepatic hilum, exposing lymph node station twelve alongside the hepatic artery. Clips are used to control lymphatic vessels to prevent leakage. The dissection continues alongside the portal vein using a coagulating hook, and lymph nodes at station five are taken. The dissection extends to station eight along the hepatic artery.

The surgeon performs upper dissection of the lesser omentum, targeting lymph node station one. Dissection of the left gastric artery and vein is carried out, followed by cephalic dissection reaching lymph node station seven and then station one. The upper left stomach is dissected, with attention to the left gastroepiploic vein and artery, which are challenging to locate in obese patients. The gastrosplenic ligament is dissected, avoiding the splenic hilum to prevent bleeding. Traction of the stomach to the right exposes lymph node station two, completing the lymph node dissection. The last few centimeters of the esophagus are freed from adhesions to expose the lower third for stapling.

The surgeon completes the dissection near the esophagus, ensuring not to extend too deeply into the thorax due to the vascular supply from the aorta. The specimen is retrieved through a Pfannenstiel incision. The angle of Treitz is identified, and the jejunum is lifted in preparation for the esophagojejunostomy.

The surgeon uses a blue cartridge stapler to perform a linear lateral anastomosis, with the assistant's trocar stretching the limb to ensure precise alignment. Two half running sutures with PDS 2-0 are tied together at the midpoint of the anterotomy. A 60-centimeter alimentary limb is measured, and a laterolateral jejuno-jejunal anastomosis is performed using a linear stapler with a white cartridge. The anterotomy is closed with two half running sutures.

The surgeon divides the ozojejunal and jejunojejunal anastomoses. An aortic test is performed with the anesthesiologist's assistance. The oestrogen anastomosis is fixed to the right crus using 2-0 silk sutures. The 12mm trocar port is closed, and a drainage tube is left in place.
